speaker: interviewer
text: Is it all right? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's always going to be over there. Do you want something like a plant or?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. [? There's good. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, there's good?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, there's good. OK. Need to fill this in. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the grandmother I told you about. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date, March 15, 1995, survivor Lilly Wolf, interviewer, Danielle Klein, city of Sydney, Australia. And it's in English. OK. My name is Dani Klein, D-A-N-I, Klein, K-L-E-I-N. Date today is the 15th of March, 1995. And I will be interviewing the survivor Lilly Wolf in Sydney, Australia, in English. Can you-- Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lilly Wolf at present. Born--

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-double L-Y and Wolf, W-O-L-F.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. And did you have a different name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was Baruch, B-A-R-U-C-H. And my birth-- my name on my birth certificate was Livia Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-V-I-A, and Anna, A-double N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. And were you ever known by any other names, like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was married once in Hungary. And my husband's name was Weinreb-- W-E-I-N-R-E-B. And then we changed it to Varadi, Hungarianized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-A-R-A-D-I. But we came out again to Australia. I can't even remember, was it Varadi or Weinreb, one of the two. I think we changed it back to Weinreb--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because it was easier to pronounce it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you ever known by any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child I had a lot of freckles. And they called me in school, Szepi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-E-P-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freckleface.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Freckly. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, but I can't remember. Don't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 28th of the 7th. And now you get a shock-- 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is your age at the moment, Lilly?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell me the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city was Budapest. And the country was Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. OK. Can you tell me, Lilly, who comprised your household before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents, my brother, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Otto Baruch. And my mother was Margaret [? Muncie. ?] And her maiden name was Weiszenstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you had a brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a brother who was born 14 months after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fredi-- Alfred, Alfred Joseph Baruch. And he perished, unfortunately, in the years of '45, January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, OK. We'll get to that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little bit later. OK, so you lived in, in a house with, with your parents and your brother. Can you tell me a little bit about your, your life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well actually I had a very hectic early age. My parents, my father, was a manager for a shoe manufacturers in Czechoslovakia, which was called [INAUDIBLE]. And his job was to go to North Africa and establish a shop, rent a-- a premises, furnish it, run it in, employ people to do it. So that when-- he-- after he left, that was a going concern. Now we did that for three years-- one year in Algier, one year in Oran, and Tangier. And I was conceived in Africa. And then my mother wanted me to be born and have Hungarian citizenship, so we came back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave birth to me. And then after six weeks, I-- we went back to Budapest. And there, she conceived my brother. And again, she came back to Hungary, gave birth to Fredi. With me-- I was with her-- and we went back to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Africa. And we were there until I was about three and 1/2, four years old. Then we came back. So actually, my mother tongue is French. Because they spoke French in North Africa. And on the way back-- we had to leave Africa, because my brother didn't-- the, the weather, the, the climate, didn't agree with him, and the food what was there. So we came back all together. My father gave up that particular job for the firm. And they have given him a shop in-- well, actually a whole building-- and the bottom part, shop part-- in Wunstorf, which was Sudeten, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, we lived until-- that was a very happy part of my life-- we lived until-- I went to school there till I was about eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- you got to Sudeten, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany after Africa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been around about four years old, something like that. Because I think we spent some time in Budapest with the family and so on. And I must have been about four years old, something like that. I can't remember exactly. But in Wunstorf, where we lived, that was, as I said, very, very pleasant area. And we stayed there until I was 10-- nine, nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then for a year, I had to live with my grandmother in Bratislava, [INAUDIBLE], which is a border--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK, before we talk about that, when you were in Wunstorf, were-- did you go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to a German language school. And it's funny that those first two years or three years of schooling in-- two years of schooling-- in Wunstorf, I still cannot say the ABC any other way but in the German. Well, it's been brainwashed into my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can't say it any other way. Counting is different-- counting, I do it in Hungarian or in English. But the ABC goes to me automatically in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German, really? So OK, you were at school for the first couple of years in Wunstorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, in Wunstorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember the memories that you have of being at school at that time? Was there any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Funny, I don't remember anything of school time in Wunstorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's more our private life, which was very pleasant. I did not feel any anti-Semitism. I only know that we belonged to a certain middle-class crowd, where my brother and I were always dressed the same way. We had the same Austrian uniform. Or we had the same Scottish uniform, she in a, he in the little pants and I in a skirt. But the hat were the same and the blouses. And I've still got photos where we, both of us, were photographed in the same sort of outfit. And I can, I can remember we were once in a teddy bear outfit. You know, these are the little memories which are coming back to me. I can see myself. And I looked at my brother. And I thought, isn't he cute. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, lovely, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that-- those years were very, very pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have many memories of, of your private life, as opposed to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: School years, I don't remember at all. I don't remember a teacher. I do know we had a nanny, my brother and I. And in the beginning I didn't like her much. She was too authoritarian. And I rebelled against it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was also in Wunstorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was all in Wunstorf. But eventually, I think I [LAUGHS] mellowed down to her and did what she was telling us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stefi. Yeah, she was very nice lady. I have photos of her, too, funny enough, after so many years. Anyway, these were nice times in Wunstorf. And then, as I said, that was already close to '38-- '37, '37, '36. And I had to go over for one year to my grandma, my paternal grandmother. They lived in Bratislava, in [INAUDIBLE], with her daughter and her son-in-law. And I lived there for a year, which was very pleasant as well. But I didn't have none of my parents or my brother with me. I was alone with my grandma sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was that, Lilly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember exactly. I think my mother came back to Hungary with my brother, who wasn't-- he was healthy by that time. But he was always somehow physically the weaker one out of the two. And now with common sense, I think that the birth, after 14 months of the first child, me, who was a very healthy, strong girl, he suffered by my mother's not giving herself enough time between one and the second child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she came back to Budapest. And I stayed with Grandma. Why, what was the background, I don't really know, never questioned it. And I didn't see my father much after that, because after Bratislava, I came to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school while you were in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any-- do you have any memories of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of the schools, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, is that a [LAUGHS] block, a mental block against schools? On the other hand, I remember when we got-- when I got back to Budapest, I couldn't speak any Hungarian at all. They made me, more or less, forget French. Because I had to concentrate on German to be good in school in Wunstorf. So automatically, my French was totally neglected. And when I came back to Budapest, they put me in a boarding school, where I was sort of transformed from German into Hungarian language.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: About nine, nine and 1/2, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I can't remember was I there for a year, or nine months, or 15 months? That, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were back in Budapest. At this stage, were you reunited with your mother, and father, and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but, but I was in the boarding school, live-in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't reunited as a family. I, I most probably-- that, I can't remember, either-- I was visited on the weekend. Or they picked me up for the weekend. It was in Budapest, in the city, so it wasn't-- it shouldn't have been any hardship to pick me up or to visit me. But I can't recall any of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And do you have any memories of, of what school was like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, once again?

speaker: interviewee
text: None, none whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At that point, when you were about nine or ten, do you have any memories of what-- whether there was any anti-Semitism in your life when you were in Hungary at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, somehow I was never conscious-- only later in years-- I was never conscious of anti-Semitic remarks, only later on in schools, but not in the beginning, not-- neither in Wunstorf nor in the boarding school. After the boarding school, with my brother together, we had to go to a country boarding school. Because what happened was that my grandmother, my maternal grandmother, has given us a home in her apartment with my auntie together, my mother's sister. And she hadn't-- didn't have enough room for all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother Fredi and I went into the country to another boarding school. Believe it or not, it was a nunnery. So I was staying in the girl's department. And my brother was staying in the priestly department. And we, we were there for about a year and a half to two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they-- was the school aware that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes, very much aware, so much so that when Christmas came, all the children went with their parents but us. Fredi and I were staying in the school, in the boarding school. And there, I have some pleasant and unpleasant memories. So by then, I was fully conscious of what was going on. And we had some remarks thrown at us that we were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was about-- '37, '38, '39, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And when you say that there were remarks thrown at you that you were Jews, would that have been from the teachers or the students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah-- not students, teachers. The-- well, being a nunnery, I don't know, and I can't recall, any strong derogatory remarks. But there was a difference between a Jewish girl and a non-Jewish girl, if you know what I mean. They have made a remark, or they called you names, which weren't over-nasty, but they made a difference whether you are Jewish or not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware already then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I was aware. And I was aware that, that we were somehow second-rate citizens, if not a third.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about whether you have any memories of what Jewish life was like in Hungary at that time or what the Jewish community was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Look, I was 10, 11 years old, I wasn't very conscious of it. I can't remember. I know that my parents and grandmother had problems. And I overheard certain worries they had, and remarks, and so on. But it was never somehow discussed with us. Somehow, they wanted to protect us. Or they think-- they thought that we couldn't give any positive suggestions what to do and how to do. So we weren't involved in any discussions. But of course, we heard that they were listening to the radios, or the war, where, what, and how.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so this is already once the war was kind of on-- brewing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not in Hungary, in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm wondering, though, whether, whether you have any descriptions of just generally, quite aside from the war, what, what sort of Jewish life was like in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you what we felt, that the Jews were losing slowly their, their academic position, that kids couldn't go to university. Of course, numerus clausus and numerus nullus came after, after that. But that Jews could not keep the position in their firm. Let's say they had a big factory. It was taken away from them nice and slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, there were laws which were brought, which were brought out against the Jews, meaning they were restricted. They can't work in a bank. Or they can't be a general manager in any of the firms. They can't have their own big firms, because it interfered with the Hungarian economic life and, and export, or whatever. Of course by that time we heard that a lot of Hungarian Jews go overseas and try to rescue themselves and the families. Lilly, what about in-- in-- sort of Jewish observances and rituals? And, and would you describe the Jewish community in Hungary? Was it relatively kind of religious? Or were they-- I mean, or-- or let's say specifically your family. What, what kind of Jewish rituals and traditions do you remember keeping as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we were very liberal. I must agree that we didn't keep a Friday night. We didn't observe it at all. On the other hand, both my brothers and my became bar mitzvah and bat mitzvah. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Both your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] No, both my brother and I became bar mitzvah and bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was more so a pushing of my uncle-- a great-uncle, my grandmother's cousin, I think, twice removed-- who said that he definitely-- he was a religious man and had a kosher household, and financially, very nicely liquid. So he supported wherever he could. And he pushed his foot down that the two of us have to have our bat mitzvah and bar mitzvah. Otherwise, we only had the two to three high holy days in the year, which as you know, the Pesach, and Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur was always there. As a matter of fact-- well, I will tell you after. The Yom Kippur, I had a very nasty experience. After the war, they were-- I kept the Yom Kippur. And I collapsed in the toilet about 3:00 o'clock in the afternoon. And I was forever and ever after taken off-- I'm not allowed to diet, meaning fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family did keep things like Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, oh, yes, high holy days we always kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't have the Friday night rituals, or the Saturday morning going to synagogue, or anything like that. We did pick up the telephone. And in Budapest, there were mixed and majority very [? neoliberal ?] people, where there are-- there were quite a few who were very religious and had a kosher household. But these things were more in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city, big city, people were more liberal. They had the few synagogues where they went to. There were yeshivas in our area as well, because we lived in a, let's say, the equivalent of Bondi here. It was a Jewish area, but it wasn't a typical-- the street where we lived, it wasn't a Jewish street. It's only behind it was a Jewish area. So if we wanted to, we did go to synagogues. But we, as I said, us ourselves were not religious in that respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you, for instance, when you were growing up, let's say, in some of the other places like Wunstorf and so on, was it also easy for you as a family to maintain your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but there I don't remember having a Pesach or a Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wunstorf. Now, I don't know whether was that on purpose, or my parents. I haven't got any idea. Whether they were afraid that-- of course by that time Hitler existed. And seeing that it was a Sudeten German township, they didn't want to rock the boat and tell everybody that we're Jewish, I don't know. I really don't know, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family, and-- and-- and are your memories, are-- are they such that most of their friends and people they, sort of, socialized with, were they mainly fellow Jews, or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't know. Not in Wunstorf-- in Budapest we were already with Jewish friends, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. So, so there was very much a community feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I remember that when we came back from that country boarding school into Budapest, we had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a housekeeper who was, naturally, Christian. And she was very, very decent, our homekeeper. But in the block of units, there was a caretaker. And he was a bit of a Nazi. He had a young son who took a fancy to me and I to him. By that time, I was 13-- 12, 13, 14. And I remember that we had to hide in the elevator up and down to-- to steal a kiss here and there. [LAUGHTER] But he wasn't allowed to be seen with me, because I was Jewish and he wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So that was already in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was back in Budapest, I would say in '40, '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were aware as a, as a young teenager that that was because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: My word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Of course, as I told you before, the laws came in every six months, something new that the Jews are not allowed to do that. The Jewish children are not allowed to go to the-- certain schools, because they don't need education or whatever. So they-- we had quite a lot of restrictions by that time. In the beginning, I don't remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lilly, did you belong to a Jewish youth organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. And that was before the Hungarian war. And it was called Omike-- O-M-I-K-E. It was a Jewish organization of-- I don't know what the ones in Sydney are, in Australia, Betar-- where they got together on Sunday for a dance, or for going out into the countryside, or having a picnic, or things like that. And I remember I had-- by that time, I was 14, 14, 15. And I remember. I had a boyfriend who-- of course, boyfriend in those days was not going to bed with a guy, but to go out, that he came up to your home and asked your mother's permission to take you out to a movie, or to a dance, or to a meal, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was my boyfriend. He was a pianist, a very good one on top of it. And he was our group's pianist when we had a, when we had a dancing group. And of course, I always missed out to dance with him. So he got a radiogram-- is that what they called it? A phonogram.

speaker: interviewee
text: Phonogram, and put on a few records, and then he just grabbed me. And we went dancing. So these were little memory. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Klein-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? Lesley ?] Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what's happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did not survive. Yeah, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but that-- those were, again, memories which I already aware of, very much so. And I remember, I brought home a little puppy whom I found on the street. And, and my grandmother throw me out with, with the dog together, sort of. We can't afford to have a dog in the, in the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point you were actually living with your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my mother was not around anymore. She passed on. So that's all--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so your mother actually died before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you call war then? The war started with me in '39. In 1939, I was, as you know, a young kid of 12 years-- yeah. And that was the last time I saw my father. Because he was born originally in Vienna, in Austria. And he was not a Hungarian state person. So when the Germans were tightening, or the Hungarian Nazis, the [NON-ENGLISH], were tightening the grip on the Hungarian Jews, he was one of them who wasn't allowed to stay in Hungary because he wasn't a Hungarian citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he tried to get a permit or a visa into several countries in Europe. And Belgium was the only place where they let him in. Now, he spoke six languages. That meant that French was a fluent language for him as well. And he had no fear to survive in Brussels, in Belgium. And he, he played a beautiful jazz piano and-- not classical as much. But he did play classical as well. And he went as a pianist into Belgium, to Brussels. And he lived there for-- from '39 till '42. And he always tried to, to send some money home to Budapest. My mother was working as a secretary, clerical secretary. And my brother and I went to school. By that time, it got-- we got back to Budapest, that was after the boarding schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you were going to school in Budapest at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: As well, by then in a public school, which was very close to us. It was just a normal public school where Jews were there and non-Jews. And I lost the thread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you living at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Grandma and my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Was that your paternal or maternal grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maternal-- my paternal stayed in Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my maternal was the one where my brother, Fredi, and my mother came-- my father, for all I know-- to Hungary from Wunstorf, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed with Grandma-- yeah, the paternal grandma. So we stayed in, in Budapest with my maternal relatives, with my mother. My father wasn't there anymore, right? He went to Brussels. And we went to school until I was 14. In the meantime, my auntie gave birth to a daughter, Vera, whom I adored. I, I really always, my ambition was to get-- to become a mother, to push the pram of a baby. I somehow felt I had so much affection and love to give to someone that that little baby in the pram was the only one whom I can give it to. Because my mother was a very sick person. She had angina pectoris, which meant that she had a problem with the heart-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how you say that in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sort of a very weak heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very weak heart-- as a child she had scarlet fever. And that was left over from a weak heart. And of course, the knowledge that her husband wasn't with her and that it didn't look very kosher in those days, the Hungarian situation with the Jews. She always was afraid that she won't see him anymore. And she went down the hill emotionally. And with emotional came the sickness. And she died a natural-- well, natural death-- in 1943. Yes. So she, she lived to see her sister's baby, Vera, whom she loved very much. And after that, as I said, she died. And I stayed with my brother at the family's premises in the apartment.
